title: Polymorphic Collections
summary: Learn how a service can loop through mixed subclass objects and call the same method on each one through a shared base-class contract.

sketchBlocks.title: One loop, many object types
Shared contract, different results
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A polymorphic collection works because each object promises the same method name, but each subclass can return its own version of the result.

Here is a worked example with report lines:

```python
class Item:
    def __init__(self, title):
        self.title = title

class Book(Item):
    def summary_line(self):
        return f"BOOK | {self.title}"

class Game(Item):
    def summary_line(self):
        return f"GAME | {self.title}"
```

Even though both methods are named `summary_line()`, they format the text differently.

If a service does this:

```python
items = [Book("Clean Code"), Game("Puzzle Quest")]
lines = []

for item in items:
    lines.append(item.summary_line())

print(lines)
```

The result is:

```python
['BOOK | Clean Code', 'GAME | Puzzle Quest']
```

The important idea is that the loop only knows about the shared method name. Each subclass decides what that method should return.

sketchBlocks.title: Services should depend on the base-class contract

quizDraft.title: Build a mixed item list
quizDraft.prompt: A library dashboard needs one loop that can show labels for different item types. In the provided workspace, finish the `display_name` methods so a `Book` and a `Video` can live in the same `items` list and the loop in `main.py` prints the correct labels. Expected output should be two lines: `Book: Python Patterns` and `Video: Debugging Demo`.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods.

semanticChecks.message: Define the Book class.
Define the Video class.
Book.display_name() should format the book label.
Video.display_name() should format the video label.
Create at least one Book instance in the workspace.
Create at least one Video instance in the workspace.
The program should print two lines from the loop.

quizDraft.hint: Both subclasses need the same method name so the loop can call it on each object.

help.concept: Polymorphism works when different subclasses provide the same method name, letting one loop call that method on every object in a mixed list.
help.hint_1: Open the subclass files and look for the missing `display_name` methods.
help.hint_2: Each method should return a string that includes its type label and the inherited `title` attribute.

quizDraft.title: Collect summary lines from mixed subclasses
quizDraft.prompt: A media catalog report should gather summary lines from different item types. Complete the `summary_line` methods in the provided subclass files so `services/reporting.py` returns a list with `BOOK | Clean Code` and `GAME | Puzzle Quest` when it loops over the mixed objects.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods.

semanticChecks.message: Book.summary_line() should return the expected report text.
Game.summary_line() should return the expected report text.
The reporting service should build lines by looping over mixed items.
Running the workspace should print the two summary lines.

quizDraft.hint: The service already loops correctly; the missing behavior belongs in the subclasses.

help.concept: In a polymorphic collection, the service can stay generic while each subclass customizes the shared method result.
help.hint_1: Check `services/reporting.py` to see which method name the loop calls.
help.hint_2: Return different prefixes in each subclass, but keep the same method name in both classes.

quizDraft.title: Write a generic pricing service
quizDraft.prompt: A shop report should work with mixed item types without checking each subclass name. In the provided workspace, finish the `price_tag` methods and the `build_tags(items)` service so it returns price tags for both a `Book` and a `Course`. When the workspace runs, the output should include `Python 101 - $12` and `Team Training - $25` on separate lines.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods and service.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the missing methods and service.

semanticChecks.message: Book.price_tag() should return the formatted price tag.
Course.price_tag() should return the formatted price tag.
Running the workspace should print two tags.

quizDraft.hint: The service should loop over `items` and call the same method on each object.

help.concept: A service that depends on a shared base-class contract can work with many subclasses by calling one common method inside a loop.
help.hint_1: Look for the shared method name used by both subclasses and the service.
help.hint_2: Each subclass should return its own formatted tag, and `build_tags` should append those results to a list.

quizDraft.title: Why one loop can handle mixed subclasses
quizDraft.prompt: Why can a single loop call the same method on both `Book` and `Video` objects in one list?
options: Because Python automatically turns every object into the same class inside a list
Because both subclasses provide the same method name that the loop calls
Because lists can only store objects with identical attributes
Because the loop checks each object type before every method call
quizDraft.hint: Think about what both objects must provide for the loop to work.

help.concept: Polymorphism depends on shared behavior: different subclasses can be used the same way when they follow the same base-class contract.
help.hint_1: The loop does not need matching subclass names; it needs a shared method name.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about both classes defining the same callable behavior.

quizDraft.title: Complete the shared method call
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing method name used by a polymorphic report loop.
quizDraft.hint: Use the method name that every object in the mixed list is expected to have.

help.concept: A polymorphic loop succeeds when every object supports the same method name from the shared contract.
help.hint_1: The loop is not choosing between different method names.
help.hint_2: Pick the method name that belongs to the shared reporting behavior, not a class name or keyword.

quizDraft.title: Choose the polymorphism-friendly service patterns
quizDraft.prompt: Which service patterns fit polymorphic collections? Choose all that apply.
options: Loop through mixed objects and call `display_name()` on each one
Write one branch for `Book`, another for `Video`, and another for every future subclass
Pass a mixed list into a service that builds report lines from a shared method
Require the service to rename methods so each subclass uses a different one
quizDraft.hint: Look for choices where the service depends on shared behavior instead of exact subclass checks.

help.concept: Polymorphic services stay generic by calling shared methods on mixed objects, which makes the code easier to extend.
help.hint_1: A good service loop usually calls one shared method on each object.
help.hint_2: Select the options that avoid hard-coding separate branches for every subclass.

quizDraft.title: Order the steps in a polymorphic service
quizDraft.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for building report lines from a mixed object list.
tokens: Receive a list of mixed item objects
Loop through each item in the list
Call the shared method such as `summary_line()`
Append each returned string to the results list
correctOrder: Receive a list of mixed item objects
Loop through each item in the list
Call the shared method such as `summary_line()`
Append each returned string to the results list
quizDraft.hint: Start with the collection, then loop, then call the shared method, then store the result.

help.concept: A polymorphic service usually follows a simple sequence: receive mixed objects, iterate, call shared behavior, and collect results.
help.hint_1: The method call happens inside the loop, not before the list exists.
help.hint_2: Think about how a `lines` list gets filled one object at a time.

quizDraft.title: What should the service depend on?
quizDraft.prompt: When writing a service for a mixed list of subclasses, what should the service depend on most?
options: The order in which the subclasses were defined
The shared base-class contract and method names
Whether every object has exactly the same attribute values
A separate loop for each subclass type
quizDraft.hint: Think about the shared promise between the subclasses.

help.concept: A polymorphic service should rely on the base-class contract or shared method names, not on exact subclass-specific branching.
help.hint_1: The service should care about behavior that every object in the list supports.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer about the shared contract rather than subclass-by-subclass checks.

quizDraft.title: Complete the mixed collection example
quizDraft.prompt: Fill in the missing variable name for the mixed collection passed into a service.
quizDraft.hint: Use the collection variable that a service would loop over.

help.concept: Polymorphic services usually accept one collection of mixed objects and iterate through it using shared behavior.
help.hint_1: The blank is the list variable, not a class name or method name.
help.hint_2: Choose the plural collection name that matches a list of objects.

quizDraft.title: Add a new subclass without changing the service
quizDraft.prompt: Your catalog app now needs a `Magazine` item. Starting from the existing workspace, add the new subclass so the same reporting service can include it without any new type-checking logic. When the workspace runs, it should print three lines for a book, a video, and a magazine.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and add the new subclass.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and add the new subclass.

semanticChecks.message: Define the Magazine class.
Magazine should be constructible with a title.
Magazine.display_name() should match the shared contract.
Running the workspace should print three labels.

quizDraft.hint: Follow the same shared method contract that the existing subclasses already use.

help.concept: A strong polymorphic design lets you add new subclasses by implementing the shared method, while the service loop stays unchanged.
help.hint_1: Inspect the existing subclasses and copy their overall structure with a different label.
help.hint_2: Make sure `Magazine` inherits from the same base class and defines the same report method the service already calls.

quizDraft.title: Build a checkout summary from mixed rentals
quizDraft.prompt: A rental desk stores `BookRental` and `MovieRental` objects in one list. Starting from the provided workspace, complete the shared `checkout_line()` methods and the service that joins them into one summary string separated by newlines. The final output should show one line per rental in the mixed list.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the rental summary behavior.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the rental summary behavior.

semanticChecks.message: BookRental.checkout_line() should return the expected line.
MovieRental.checkout_line() should return the expected line.
Running the workspace should print one line per rental.

quizDraft.hint: Each subclass should provide the same method name, and the service should loop once.

help.concept: Polymorphism lets a service combine output from different subclasses by calling one shared method on each object in a mixed collection.
help.hint_1: Look for the method name the service is supposed to call on every rental object.
help.hint_2: Return the formatted line in each subclass, then collect those lines in order inside the service.

quizDraft.title: Create a stock alert service for mixed inventory items
quizDraft.prompt: A store tracks `BookItem` and `SupplyItem` objects together. Starting from the provided workspace, complete the shared `alert_line()` methods and the service that returns only the alert lines for items whose quantity is below 3. The service should still work by calling the same method on each object in the mixed list.
quizDraft.starterCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the low-stock alert behavior.
quizDraft.solutionCode: # Open the provided workspace files and complete the low-stock alert behavior.

semanticChecks.expected: LOW STOCK: Book API Design has 2 left
semanticChecks.message: BookItem.alert_line() should format the alert text.

semanticChecks.expected: LOW STOCK: Supply Sticky Notes has 1 left
semanticChecks.message: SupplyItem.alert_line() should format the alert text.
Running the workspace should print the low-stock alerts.

quizDraft.hint: The filtering can check quantity, but the formatted alert text should come from the shared method on each object.

help.concept: A polymorphic service can combine shared behavior with simple filtering: it loops through mixed objects, selects the ones that match a condition, and calls the same method on each selected object.
help.hint_1: Find where quantity is stored, then decide which objects should be included before calling the shared method.
help.hint_2: Both subclasses should define `alert_line()`, and the service should append those results only for low-stock items.

projectDraft.title: Catalog services with mixed item objects
